pattern: A length-five palindrome is fixed by its first two and last two digits, so counting prefix and suffix digit-pair frequencies around each center yields the total in linear time.
hints: A five-character palindrome looks like a b c b a, so its shape is decided by the outer two-digit pattern on each side and a free middle.
For each possible center position, you need how many times each ordered digit pair appears to its left and the mirrored pair to its right.
Maintain rolling counts of two-digit prefixes ending before index i and two-digit suffixes starting after i; for each center i and each pair xy, add prefix[xy] times suffix[xy], all modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: two disjoint runs give exactly two

stress.description: random short digit strings within brute-force reach
stress.note: kept under ~40 chars so the C(n,5) oracle stays fast

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def countPalindromes(self, s):
        MOD = 10**9 + 7
        n = len(s)
        digits = '0123456789'
        pairs = [a + b for a in digits for b in digits]
        suffix = {p: 0 for p in pairs}
        suffix_single = [0] * 10
        ans = 0
        # build full suffix pair counts first (pairs i<j to the right)
        # we sweep center from right to left, maintaining suffix pairs for indices > center
        # prefix pairs for indices < center maintained on the fly
        # We'll do two passes: first compute suffix pair counts for each center.
        # suffixPairCount[c][pair] is heavy; instead sweep.
        # Right-to-left build of suffix single and pair counts.
        suf_pair = [None] * (n + 1)
        cnt_single = [0] * 10
        cnt_pair = {p: 0 for p in pairs}
        suf_pair[n] = dict(cnt_pair)
        for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            d = int(s[i])
            for x in range(10):
                # pair starting with s[i] then a later single digit x
                cnt_pair[s[i] + digits[x]] += cnt_single[x]
            cnt_single[d] += 1
            suf_pair[i] = dict(cnt_pair)
        pre_single = [0] * 10
        pre_pair = {p: 0 for p in pairs}
        for c in range(n):
            sp = suf_pair[c + 1]
            for p in pairs:
                rev = p[1] + p[0]
                if pre_pair[p] and sp[rev]:
                    ans += pre_pair[p] * sp[rev]
            # update prefix with s[c]
            d = int(s[c])
            for x in range(10):
                pre_pair[digits[x] + s[c]] += pre_single[x]
            pre_single[d] += 1
        return ans % MOD
